---
title: "Run Your First Line of Code"
subtitle: "Look at a real dataset, pull out a column, and make your first plot, right in your browser"
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light: [minty, ../../../../theme-light.scss]
      dark: [quartz, ../../../../theme-dark.scss]
    css: ../../../../styles.css
    include-in-header: ../../../../_includes/skip-nav.html
    page-layout: article
    fontsize: 1.2em
    toc: false
    math: false
    grid:
      sidebar-width: 200px
      body-width: 1000px
      margin-width: 200px
    webr:
      cell-options:
        min-lines: 4
        caption: "R code block"
engine: knitr
# Site-wide _quarto.yml sets `execute: enabled: false` (static build). Quarto Live's
# _knitr.qmd include has an {r} setup block that MUST run (needs R) to turn {webr}
# blocks into interactive widgets, so re-enable execution for this page only. The
# {webr} blocks themselves still run client-side in the browser, not at build time.
# `freeze: auto` caches the render output in _freeze/ so Netlify (which has no R)
# publishes from the cache instead of re-executing. Run `quarto render` on this file
# locally after every edit, and commit _freeze/, before pushing.
execute:
  enabled: true
  freeze: auto
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ../../../../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

```{=html}
<style>
/* Night-mode look for the interactive webR editors, even while the page is in light mode.
   Only overrides CSS variables the extension already uses, scoped to the editor card,
   so prose and static solution snippets are untouched. */
.card.exercise-editor {
  --exercise-code-block-bg: #1e1e2e;
  --exercise-code-block-color: #e6e7ee;
  --exercise-main-color: #e6e7ee;
  --exercise-cap-bg: #181825;
  --exercise-gray: #3b3b52;
  --exercise-primary-rgb: 137, 180, 250;
  --exercise-line-bg: rgba(137, 180, 250, .12);
  --exercise-line-gutter-bg: rgba(137, 180, 250, .22);
  /* light-on-dark syntax palette */
  --exercise-editor-hl-kw: #ff9e64;
  --exercise-editor-hl-cf: #ff9e64;
  --exercise-editor-hl-st: #9ece6a;
  --exercise-editor-hl-ss: #9ece6a;
  --exercise-editor-hl-ch: #9ece6a;
  --exercise-editor-hl-dv: #ffc777;
  --exercise-editor-hl-bn: #ffc777;
  --exercise-editor-hl-fl: #ffc777;
  --exercise-editor-hl-cn: #ffc777;
  --exercise-editor-hl-fu: #82aaff;
  --exercise-editor-hl-va: #c0caf5;
  --exercise-editor-hl-op: #89ddff;
  --exercise-editor-hl-ot: #89ddff;
  --exercise-editor-hl-at: #7dcfff;
  --exercise-editor-hl-co: #9399b2;
  --exercise-editor-hl-er: #f7768e;
  border: 1px solid #3b3b52;
  border-radius: 10px;
  overflow: hidden;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, .18);
}
/* The header (with its "R code block" label) sits on a dark bar now, so make its
   text and outline buttons light for contrast. */
.card.exercise-editor .card-header {
  color: #e6e7ee;
}
.card.exercise-editor .card-header .btn.btn-outline-dark {
  --bs-btn-color: #e6e7ee;
  --bs-btn-border-color: #6c7086;
  --bs-btn-hover-color: #1e1e2e;
  --bs-btn-hover-bg: #e6e7ee;
  --bs-btn-hover-border-color: #e6e7ee;
  --bs-btn-active-color: #1e1e2e;
  --bs-btn-active-bg: #e6e7ee;
  --bs-btn-active-border-color: #e6e7ee;
}
</style>
```

Welcome. Everything on this page runs real R, right inside your browser. Nothing to install, and you cannot break anything. If a line produces an error, that is normal: read it, adjust, and run again.

The first time you click **Run Code**, your browser downloads R once (a few seconds). After that it is quick.

::: {.callout-note}
## The two ideas behind almost every line of R

Writing R comes down to just two things.

An **object** is a named box that stores something. You make one with the arrow `<-`.

```r
x <- 9      # the name x now holds the value 9
```

A **function** is a named command that takes an input and hands back a result. You use it by putting the input in parentheses.

```r
sqrt(9)     # the sqrt function takes 9 and returns 3
```

You can also run a function on an **object** you already made. R reads the name, finds what is stored there, and uses that as the input.

```r
sqrt(x)     # same idea: sqrt takes x (which is 9) and returns 3
```

That is the whole game: you store things in **objects**, and you act on them with **functions**. Everything below is practice with those two moves.
:::

## 1. Look at the data

R comes with `iris` built in: measurements of 150 iris flowers from three species. Each flower has four measurements (sepal length and width, petal length and width) plus its species.

Click **Run Code** to see the first few rows.

```{webr}
head(iris)
```

Every row is one flower. Every column is one kind of measurement. `head()` is a *function*: it shows the top of the data so you are not flooded with all 150 rows at once.

## 2. Grab a single column

You pull one column out of a dataset with a dollar sign: `iris$Petal.Length` means "the `Petal.Length` column from `iris`." Run this to see all 150 petal-length values.

```{webr}
iris$Petal.Length
```

That is a lot of numbers. It would be much easier to work with if it had a short name of its own. That is what an object is for.

## 3. Store the column in an object

An **object** is a named box that holds a value, made with the assignment arrow `<-`. Store the petal-length column in an object named `petal_length` by filling in the column name, then click **Run Code**.

```{webr}
#| exercise: make_column_object
petal_length <- iris$______
```

::: {.hint exercise="make_column_object"}
::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint

The column is called `Petal.Length` (capital P, capital L, with a dot). It goes right after the dollar sign:

```r
petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length
```
:::
:::

::: {.solution exercise="make_column_object"}
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Solution

```r
petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length
```

Now the name `petal_length` refers to all 150 petal-length values. Nothing prints, and that is expected: assigning a value stores it quietly.
:::
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: make_column_object
#| check: true
if (!exists("petal_length", envir = .envir_result, inherits = FALSE)) {
  list(
    correct = FALSE,
    type = "warning",
    message = "I don't see an object called `petal_length` yet. Fill the blank with the column name so the line reads `petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length`."
  )
} else {
  v <- get("petal_length", envir = .envir_result)
  if (identical(v, iris$Petal.Length)) {
    list(
      correct = TRUE,
      type = "success",
      message = "That's it. `petal_length` now holds all 150 petal-length measurements, ready to use by name."
    )
  } else if (is.numeric(v) && length(v) == 150) {
    list(
      correct = FALSE,
      type = "info",
      message = "That's a real column from iris, but not the one we're after. Use `Petal.Length` (capital P, capital L, with a dot)."
    )
  } else {
    list(
      correct = FALSE,
      type = "error",
      message = "Not quite. Take the `Petal.Length` column out of `iris` with a dollar sign: `petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length`."
    )
  }
}
```

## 4. Use a function to summarize it

A **function** takes an input and hands back a result. You already used `head()` above. Here is `mean()`, which returns the average of all the values in your object.

Run this as-is. Then click at the end of the last line, press **Return**, and add a line or two of your own. Some functions worth trying: `median(petal_length)` for the middle value, `sd(petal_length)` for the standard deviation, `max(petal_length)` and `min(petal_length)` for the extremes. Functions can even wrap each other, like `sqrt(mean(petal_length))`.

```{webr}
petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length

# The mean (average) petal length:
mean(petal_length)

# Your turn: press Return at the end of a line and try another function,
# for example median(petal_length) or sd(petal_length)
```

## 5. Turn your object into a plot

Now visualize it. `hist()` is a function that draws a **histogram**, a picture of how often each range of values shows up. Put your object inside `hist()`, then click **Run Code**.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: plot_column
petal_length <- iris$Petal.Length
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: plot_column
hist(______)
```

::: {.hint exercise="plot_column"}
::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint

Put the object you just made, `petal_length`, inside the parentheses:

```r
hist(petal_length)
```
:::
:::

::: {.solution exercise="plot_column"}
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Solution

```r
hist(petal_length)
```

You should see two clumps of flowers: one group with short petals and one with long petals. That gap is a real biological signal, the short-petaled setosa flowers standing apart from the other two species.
:::
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: plot_column
#| check: true
code <- paste(.user_code, collapse = "\n")
made_hist <- grepl("hist\\s*\\(", code)
used_obj  <- grepl("petal_length", code)
if (made_hist && used_obj) {
  list(
    correct = TRUE,
    type = "success",
    message = "Beautiful. You just turned a column of numbers into a picture. That histogram shows how petal length is distributed across all 150 flowers."
  )
} else if (made_hist && !used_obj) {
  list(
    correct = FALSE,
    type = "info",
    message = "You've got `hist()`. Now put your object `petal_length` inside the parentheses so you're plotting the column you made."
  )
} else {
  list(
    correct = FALSE,
    type = "error",
    message = "Replace the blank with `petal_length`, keeping it inside `hist(...)`, so the line reads `hist(petal_length)`."
  )
}
```

## Keep playing

No grading here, and no wrong answer. Try making a different column into an object and plotting it. What does sepal length look like?

```{webr}
sepal_length <- iris$Sepal.Length
hist(sepal_length)
```

Curious how two measurements relate? This draws one flower per point, petal length against petal width:

```{webr}
plot(iris$Petal.Length, iris$Petal.Width)
```

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Try this with a partner.** One of you predicts what a plot will look like before running it; the other runs it. Then compare what you saw to what you expected, and swap. Saying your prediction out loud first is one of the fastest ways to learn.
:::
